docs.title: Anknote
docs.text: Anknote is a command-line tool that automatically generates Anki flashcards from your markdown notes using AI models. Transform your study materials into effective spaced repetition cards with minimal effort.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• 🤖 AI-Powered Generation: Uses LiteLLM to support multiple AI models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.)
  • 📝 Markdown Processing: Automatically processes markdown files and directories
  • ⚙️ Configurable: Flexible configuration via files, CLI arguments, and environment variables
  • 🔄 Batch Processing: Process multiple files at once with progress tracking
  • 📊 TSV Output: Generates Anki-compatible TSV files for easy import
  • 🛡️ Robust: Built-in error handling, retry logic, and validation
  • 🧪 Well-Tested: Comprehensive test suite with 100% coverage

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Install Anknote:

pip install anknote

Generate flashcards from a markdown file:

anknote my-notes.md

Process an entire directory:

anknote notes/ -o flashcards/

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Extract Content: Anknote reads your markdown files and extracts the content
  2. AI Processing: The content is sent to an AI model with a carefully crafted prompt
  3. Parse Cards: The AI response is parsed to extract question-answer pairs
  4. Generate TSV: Cards are saved in Anki-compatible TSV format for import

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Given a markdown file like this:

# Python Basics

Python is a high-level programming language. It was created by Guido van Rossum 
and released in 1991. Python emphasizes code readability and uses significant 
indentation.

## Key Features
- Interpreted language
- Dynamic typing
- Object-oriented programming

Anknote generates flashcards like:

  • Q: What is Python? A: A high-level programming language created by Guido van Rossum
  • Q: When was Python released? A: 1991
  • Q: What does Python emphasize? A: Code readability and uses significant indentation

docs.text:
• Installation Guide - Detailed installation instructions
  • Usage Guide - Learn all the command-line options
  • Configuration - Customize Anknote for your workflow
  • API Reference - Complete API documentation

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
All notable changes to Anknote will be documented here.

The format is based on Keep a Changelog, and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Initial release of Anknote
  • CLI tool for generating Anki flashcards from markdown notes
  • Support for multiple AI models via LiteLLM
  • Configuration management with files and environment variables
  • Comprehensive test suite with 100% coverage
  • Complete documentation with MkDocs

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Process single files or entire directories
  • Support for multiple markdown file extensions
  • TSV output format compatible with Anki
  • Retry logic with exponential backoff for API calls
  • Custom prompt file support
  • Verbose and quiet modes for different use cases

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Basic project structure
  • Core functionality for markdown processing
  • AI integration with flashcard generation
  • Command-line interface
  • Configuration system
  • Test framework

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Anknote provides flexible configuration through files, environment variables, and command-line arguments.

docs.title: Creating a Configuration File
docs.text:
Create a default configuration file:

anknote --init-config

This creates .anknote.json in your current directory with default settings:

{
  "model": "gemini/gemini-2.0-flash-lite",
  "max_retries": 3,
  "skip_readme": true,
  "file_extensions": [".md", ".markdown"],
  "output_format": "tsv",
  "escape_tabs": true,
  "log_level": "INFO"
}

docs.title: Configuration File Locations
docs.text:
Anknote looks for configuration files in this order:

  1. Current directory: .anknote.json
  2. Home directory: ~/.config/anknote/config.json
  3. XDG config: $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/anknote/config.json

docs.title: Using Custom Configuration
docs.text:
Specify a custom configuration file:

anknote notes.md --config /path/to/my-config.json

View current configuration:

anknote --show-config

docs.title: AI Model Settings
docs.text:
{
  "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
  "max_retries": 5
}
  • model: AI model identifier (supports LiteLLM format)
  • max_retries: Maximum API retry attempts

docs.title: File Processing Settings
docs.text:
{
  "skip_readme": true,
  "file_extensions": [".md", ".markdown", ".txt"]
}
  • skip_readme: Skip README files when processing directories
  • file_extensions: File extensions to process

docs.title: Output Settings
docs.text:
{
  "output_format": "tsv",
  "escape_tabs": true
}
  • output_format: Output file format (currently only "tsv")
  • escape_tabs: Escape tab characters in content

docs.title: Logging Settings
docs.text:
{
  "log_level": "DEBUG"
}
  • log_level: Logging verbosity (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR)

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Override configuration with environment variables:

export ANKNOTE_MODEL="claude-3-haiku-20240307"
export ANKNOTE_MAX_RETRIES="10"
export ANKNOTE_LOG_LEVEL="DEBUG"

Environment variables take precedence over configuration files.

docs.title: Supported AI Models
docs.text: Anknote uses LiteLLM, supporting many providers:

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text:
{
  "model": "gpt-4o-mini"
}

Set API key: export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key"

docs.title: Anthropic (Claude)
docs.text:
{
  "model": "claude-3-haiku-20240307"
}

Set API key: export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-key"

docs.title: Google (Gemini)
docs.text:
{
  "model": "gemini/gemini-2.0-flash-lite"
}

Set API key: export GOOGLE_API_KEY="your-key"

docs.title: Azure OpenAI
docs.text:
{
  "model": "azure/gpt-4o-mini"
}

Additional Azure environment variables required.

docs.title: Cost-Optimized Setup
docs.text:
For minimal costs while maintaining quality:

{
  "model": "gemini/gemini-2.0-flash-lite",
  "max_retries": 3,
  "log_level": "WARNING"
}

docs.title: High-Quality Setup
docs.text:
For maximum quality flashcards:

{
  "model": "gpt-4o",
  "max_retries": 5,
  "log_level": "INFO"
}

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
For development and debugging:

{
  "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
  "max_retries": 1,
  "log_level": "DEBUG",
  "skip_readme": false
}

docs.title: Custom File Types
docs.text:
Process additional file types:

{
  "file_extensions": [".md", ".markdown", ".txt", ".org"],
  "skip_readme": false
}

docs.title: Custom Prompts
docs.text:
Use a custom prompt file:

anknote notes.md --prompt-file my-prompt.txt

The prompt file should contain instructions for the AI model on how to generate flashcards.

docs.title: Project-Specific Configuration
docs.text:
For different projects, create project-specific configs:

# Academic project
anknote notes/ --config configs/academic.json

# Work notes
anknote work-notes/ --config configs/work.json

docs.title: Batch Configuration
docs.text:
For processing multiple directories with different settings:

#!/bin/bash
anknote academic-notes/ --config academic.json -o anki/academic/
anknote work-notes/ --config work.json -o anki/work/
anknote personal-notes/ --config personal.json -o anki/personal/

docs.title: Priority Order
docs.text:
Configuration values are applied in this order (highest to lowest priority):

  1. Command-line arguments
  2. Environment variables
  3. Configuration file
  4. Default values

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Anknote validates all configuration values:

  • Model names are checked for basic format
  • Retry counts must be positive integers
  • Log levels must be valid Python logging levels
  • File extensions must start with a dot

Invalid configurations will show an error message with suggestions for fixing them.

docs.title: Contributing to Anknote
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Anknote! This guide will help you get started with development.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.13 or higher
  • Git
  • An AI model API key for testing

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
1. Fork and clone the repository: 

    git clone https://github.com/yourusername/anknote.git
cd anknote

  2. Install development dependencies: 

    pip install -e ".[dev,docs]"

  3. Install pre-commit hooks: 

    pre-commit install

  4. Set up your AI API key: 

    export GOOGLE_API_KEY="your-key"  # or your preferred provider

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
Run the full test suite: 

pytest

Run with coverage: 

pytest --cov=anknote --cov-report=html

Run specific tests: 

pytest tests/test_config.py -v

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
We use several tools to maintain code quality:

  • Ruff: Fast Python linter and formatter
  • mypy: Static type checking
  • pre-commit: Automated code quality checks

Run quality checks manually: 

ruff check src/ tests/
ruff format src/ tests/
mypy src/

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Build documentation locally: 

uv run mkdocs serve

Build static documentation: 

uv run mkdocs build

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
anknote/
├── src/anknote/          # Main package
│   ├── __init__.py       # Package init and entry point
│   ├── main.py           # CLI interface
│   ├── config.py         # Configuration management
│   └── io/               # I/O modules
│       ├── card.py       # Data models
│       ├── gather.py     # File processing
│       └── write.py      # AI integration and output
├── tests/                # Test suite
├── docs/                 # Documentation
├── examples/             # Example configurations
└── scripts/              # Development scripts

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8 style guidelines
  • Use type hints for all functions
  • Add docstrings to all public functions and classes
  • Keep functions focused and small
  • Use descriptive variable and function names

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• Write tests for all new functionality
  • Maintain 100% test coverage
  • Use pytest fixtures for common test setup
  • Mock external dependencies (AI API calls)

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Update documentation for any user-facing changes
  • Include examples in docstrings
  • Update the changelog for notable changes

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
We use conventional commit format:

feat: add support for custom prompt files
fix: handle empty markdown files gracefully
docs: update installation instructions
test: add tests for configuration loading

Types: - feat: New features - fix: Bug fixes - docs: Documentation changes - test: Test additions or modifications - refactor: Code refactoring - perf: Performance improvements - ci: CI/CD changes

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name
  2. Make your changes: Follow the coding guidelines above
  3. Add tests: Ensure your changes are covered by tests
  4. Update documentation: Add or update relevant documentation
  5. Run quality checks: Ensure all checks pass
  6. Commit your changes: Use conventional commit format
  7. Push and create PR: Push to your fork and open a pull request

docs.title: PR Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Tests pass (pytest)
  • [ ] Code quality checks pass (ruff check, mypy)
  • [ ] Documentation is updated
  • [ ] Changelog is updated (if applicable)
  • [ ] Commit messages follow conventional format

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
Releases are automated through GitHub Actions when a new tag is pushed:

  1. Update version in pyproject.toml
  2. Update CHANGELOG.md
  3. Create and push a tag: git tag v1.0.0 && git push origin v1.0.0

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Check existing issues and discussions
  • Ask questions in GitHub Discussions
  • Review the documentation
  • Look at the test suite for examples

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: Please be respectful and inclusive in all interactions. We follow the Python community's code of conduct.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.13 or higher
  • An API key for your chosen AI model (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.)

docs.title: Install from PyPI
docs.text:
The easiest way to install Anknote is from PyPI:

pip install anknote

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
For development or to get the latest features:

git clone https://github.com/rayanramoul/anknote.git
cd anknote
pip install -e .

docs.title: Development Installation
docs.text:
If you want to contribute to Anknote:

git clone https://github.com/rayanramoul/anknote.git
cd anknote

# Install with development dependencies
pip install -e ".[dev,docs]"

# Install pre-commit hooks
pre-commit install

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Check that Anknote is installed correctly:

anknote --version

docs.title: Set Up AI Model
docs.text: Anknote uses LiteLLM, which supports many AI providers. Set up your API key:

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text: export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-api-key"

docs.title: Anthropic (Claude)
docs.text: export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-api-key"

docs.title: Google (Gemini)
docs.text: export GOOGLE_API_KEY="your-api-key"

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Create a configuration file to customize Anknote:

anknote --init-config

This creates a .anknote.json file in your current directory. See the Configuration Guide for details.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Usage Guide - Learn how to use Anknote
  • Configuration - Customize your setup

docs.title: Process a Single File
docs.text:
Generate flashcards from a markdown file:

anknote notes/python-basics.md

This creates python-basics.tsv in the current directory.

docs.title: Process Multiple Files
docs.text:
Process all markdown files in a directory:

anknote notes/ -o flashcards/

This processes all .md files in notes/ and saves the output TSV files in flashcards/.

docs.title: Input and Output
docs.text:
anknote INPUT [OPTIONS]
  • INPUT: Path to markdown file or directory
  • -o, --output DIR: Output directory (default: current directory)
  • --in-place: Save output files next to input files
  • --force-overwrite: Overwrite existing output files

docs.title: AI Model Configuration
docs.text:
anknote notes.md -m gpt-4o-mini --max-retries 5
  • -m, --model: AI model to use (default: from config)
  • --max-retries: Maximum retry attempts for API calls

docs.title: Logging and Output
docs.text:
anknote notes.md -v  # Verbose output
anknote notes.md -q  # Quiet mode
  • -v, --verbose: Enable verbose logging
  • -q, --quiet: Suppress non-error output

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
anknote --init-config          # Create default config file
anknote --show-config          # Show current configuration
anknote --config myconfig.json # Use custom config file

docs.title: Basic Processing
docs.text:
# Process a single file
anknote lecture-notes.md

# Process with custom model
anknote lecture-notes.md -m claude-3-haiku-20240307

# Process with output directory
anknote lecture-notes.md -o /path/to/anki-imports/

docs.title: Batch Processing
docs.text:
# Process all markdown files in a directory
anknote study-materials/ -o flashcards/

# Process with overwrite protection
anknote study-materials/ -o flashcards/ --force-overwrite

# In-place processing (save next to source files)
anknote study-materials/ --in-place

docs.title: Advanced Usage
docs.text:
# Use custom configuration and verbose output
anknote notes/ --config my-anknote-config.json -v

# Process with custom prompt file
anknote notes.md --prompt-file custom-prompt.txt

# Maximum retries for unreliable connections
anknote notes.md --max-retries 10

docs.title: Output Format
docs.text:
Anknote generates TSV (Tab-Separated Values) files that can be directly imported into Anki:

Question 1  Answer 1
Question 2  Answer 2
Question 3  Answer 3

docs.title: Importing to Anki
docs.text:
1. Open Anki
  2. Go to File > Import
  3. Select your generated .tsv file
  4. Configure import settings:
  5. Type: Basic
  6. Deck: Choose your target deck
  7. Fields separated by: Tab
  8. Allow HTML in fields: Yes (recommended)

docs.title: Writing Good Notes
docs.text:
For best results, structure your markdown notes clearly:

# Main Topic

Brief introduction or overview.

## Subtopic 1

Detailed explanation with key concepts.

### Important Points
- Key fact 1
- Key fact 2
- Key fact 3

## Subtopic 2

More detailed content...

docs.title: Model Selection
docs.text:
Different models have different strengths:

  • GPT-4o Mini: Fast and cost-effective for most use cases
  • GPT-4o: Higher quality but more expensive
  • Claude 3 Haiku: Fast and good for factual content
  • Gemini Pro: Good balance of speed and quality

docs.title: Processing Large Collections
docs.text:
For large note collections:

  1. Use configuration files to avoid repeating arguments
  2. Process in batches to avoid rate limits
  3. Use --verbose to monitor progress
  4. Consider using --max-retries for reliability

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
No output files generated: - Check that your markdown files contain substantial content - Verify your AI model API key is set correctly - Use --verbose to see detailed processing information

API errors: - Check your API key and quota - Try a different model - Increase --max-retries for temporary issues

Empty flashcards: - Ensure your notes have clear, factual content - Try a different AI model - Check the custom prompt if using one

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
anknote --help  # Show all options
anknote -v      # Verbose output for debugging

For more help, see the Configuration Guide or check the API Reference.
